subject-matter-expert.description: The SME role in planning transformation — knowledge transfer, process design, and capability building.

supply-and-production-data.title: Supply & Production Data
supply-and-production-data.description: Purchase orders, production orders, receipts, and real-time supply signals — the supply-side data that feeds the plan and indicates availability.

supply-planner.title: Supply Planner
supply-planner.description: Role profile for the Supply Planner — capacity constraints, supply plan ownership, and escalation.

system-roles.title: System Roles
system-roles.description: The role each system plays in the planning landscape — system of record, system of engagement, system of intelligence.

tool-landscape-overview.title: Tool Landscape
tool-landscape-overview.description: What the four systems are, why the planning landscape needs all four, and what breaks when any one is absent — the 'what exists and why' layer before diving into any system's mechanics.

tool-usage-by-role.title: Tool Usage by Role
tool-usage-by-role.description: How each planning role interacts with the tools — what they read, what they enter, and what they are responsible for.

topics.title: Who Is in the Planning Team?
topics.description: Supply chain planning doesn't happen in isolation. This topic introduces the cast of people who make planning work.

topics.title: The Planning Org Chart
topics.description: See how the planning team is structured, who reports to whom, and where cross-functional roles connect.

nodes.title: S&OP Lead
nodes.description: Owns the end-to-end planning cycle and drives cross-functional alignment.
responsibilities: Facilitate the monthly S&OP cadence
Drive consensus across demand, supply, and finance
Own the integrated business plan
Escalate unresolved planning conflicts to executive team

nodes.title: Demand Planning Manager
nodes.description: Leads the demand team and owns the demand plan output.
responsibilities: Own the statistical and consensus demand forecast
Coach demand planners on methodology and tool use
Lead the demand review meeting

nodes.title: Demand Planner
nodes.description: Generates forecasts and manages the demand signal.
responsibilities: Run the statistical baseline forecast
Incorporate market intelligence into the plan
Manage forecast exceptions and alerts
Prepare demand review materials
competencies: Data literacy and Excel/Planning software proficiency

nodes.title: Supply Planning Manager
nodes.description: Leads the supply team and owns the supply plan output.
responsibilities: Own the supply plan and capacity allocation
Coach supply planners on constraint management
Lead the supply review meeting

nodes.title: Supply Planner
nodes.description: Translates demand into feasible supply across the network.
responsibilities: Convert the demand plan into production and replenishment orders
Balance inventory targets with capacity constraints
Identify and escalate supply risks
Maintain supplier lead time and capacity data
competencies: Data accuracy and attention to detail

nodes.title: Planning Analyst
nodes.description: Maintains data quality and analytical infrastructure for the planning team.
responsibilities: Maintain master data accuracy
Build and maintain planning reports and dashboards
Support root-cause analysis on planning exceptions
Automate repetitive data tasks
competencies: Advanced data skills (SQL, Excel, BI tools)

crossFunctional.title: Finance Business Partner
crossFunctional.description: Connects the volume plan to financial performance.
responsibilities: Validate that supply chain decisions align with budget
Provide financial perspective in S&OP reviews
Own the financial reconciliation between volume and value plans

crossFunctional.title: Commercial Lead
crossFunctional.description: Brings market and customer context into the demand plan.
responsibilities: Provide sales and promotional input to the forecast
Represent customer commitments in supply discussions
Own new product launch planning from a commercial perspective

topics.title: Building an Effective Planning Team
topics.description: What separates high-performing planning teams from the rest — and the practical steps to get there.

topics.title: The Broader Organisation Structure
topics.description: The planning team sits within a larger organisational context. Understanding how the company is structured — and where planning fits — clarifies decision-making authority and stakeholder relationships.

topics.title: Quarter in the Life of a Strategic Planner
topics.description: A structured walkthrough of how a strategic planner spends a typical quarter — anchored by three monthly S&OP cycles plus a quarterly IBP or business review horizon.

topics.title: Week in the Life of an Operational Planner
topics.description: A structured walkthrough of how an operational planner spends a typical week — from Monday's order prioritisation through to Friday's execution feedback loop.

topics.title: Month in the Life of a Tactical Planner
topics.description: A structured walkthrough of how a tactical planner spends a typical month — from S&OE cadence management through to handover into the monthly S&OP cycle.

topics.title: What Is RASCI?
topics.description: RASCI is a simple framework for assigning accountability in planning processes. This topic explains what it means and why it matters.

topics.title: Demand Review RASCI
topics.description: Who owns what in the demand review step of the S&OP cycle.

topics.title: Supply Review RASCI
topics.description: Who owns what in the supply review step of the S&OP cycle.

topics.title: Executive S&OP RASCI
topics.description: Who owns what in the pre-S&OP and executive review steps of the cycle.

topics.title: Making RASCI Work in Practice
topics.description: A RASCI is only as good as the behaviour it drives. This topic covers how to implement and sustain it.

topics.title: The Authority Model
topics.description: Who can make which planning decisions, at what threshold, and what delegation looks like in practice.

topics.title: Sustaining Ownership Over Time
topics.description: How planning accountability is enforced and kept alive beyond the initial RASCI definition.

topics.title: Planning Rhythm Overview
topics.description: The S&OP and S&OE drumbeat — how the three planning horizons connect and how the cadence calendar governs when planning work happens.

topics.title: What Is S&OP?
topics.description: Sales & Operations Planning is the monthly process that aligns demand, supply, and finance into a single agreed plan — the authoritative version of what the business intends to do.

topics.title: What Is S&OE?
topics.description: Sales & Operations Execution is the weekly process that manages the near-term horizon — resolving exceptions, adjusting short-term supply, and keeping execution aligned with the S&OP plan.

topics.title: Execution: Translating Plans into Action
topics.description: Execution is where the plan becomes reality — purchase orders, production runs, shipments. Understanding how plans flow into execution systems prevents the gap between what was planned and what was done.

topics.title: The Planning Cadence
topics.description: A well-designed meeting rhythm connects monthly S&OP, weekly S&OE, and daily execution. The cadence is the operating model in practice — the recurring structure that keeps the organisation aligned.

topics.title: The Three Planning Horizons
topics.description: Planning operates across three distinct time horizons — long-term strategic, mid-term tactical, and short-term operational — each with different owners, decisions, and degrees of certainty.

topics.title: Time Fences
topics.description: Time fences define how much the plan can change within each zone of the planning horizon — protecting operational stability while keeping flexibility where it still has value.

topics.title: Horizon to Cycle Mapping
topics.description: Each planning horizon is managed by a specific process cycle — S&OP owns the mid-to-long horizon, S&OE the near-term, and execution the immediate — and decisions flow and sharpen as they move down.

topics.title: What Is Integrated Planning?
topics.description: Integrated planning means demand, supply, inventory, finance, and operations are planned together in a single connected system — not in separate silos that are reconciled after the fact.

topics.title: The Planning Hierarchy
topics.description: The planning hierarchy — strategic plan, S&OP, S&OE, execution — defines how decisions flow down from broad direction to daily action, with each level setting constraints for the one below.

topics.title: Handoffs and Dependencies
topics.description: Three critical handoffs connect the planning levels — S&OP to S&OE, S&OE to execution, and execution back to planning. Poor handoffs are the most common source of planning dysfunction.

topics.title: The S&OP Cycle
topics.description: A standard S&OP cycle runs monthly through four or five structured steps — from statistical forecast to executive sign-off.

topics.title: S&OP Roles and Participants
topics.description: S&OP requires committed participation from multiple functions. Understanding who does what — and who owns the process — is essential to making it work.

topics.title: S&OP Outputs and Decisions
topics.description: The value of S&OP is in what it produces — decisions, commitments, and a plan that the whole business can act on.

topics.title: S&OP in Planning Software
topics.description: Planning software provides the data foundation, scenario modelling, and plan publishing capabilities that make S&OP analysis faster and decisions more grounded in reality.

topics.title: Data Collection & Cleansing
topics.description: Gather sales actuals from ERP and field actuals from FMS. Remove anomalies caused by one-off events such as promotions or supply failures. Validate that all required data feeds are complete before the statistical model is run.
tasks: Extract 24 months of sales history from ERP
Extract field actuals from FMS and reconcile against ERP
Identify and flag statistical outliers for manual review
Apply promotion calendar adjustments to baseline history
Complete and sign off data completeness checklist

topics.title: Statistical Baseline Generation
topics.description: Run the statistical forecasting model on the cleansed history. Review model fit and flag product families where accuracy falls below the agreed threshold. Select the best-performing algorithm per family.
tasks: Trigger statistical forecast run in planning software
Review MAPE scores by product family
Identify families where forecast error exceeds threshold
Override algorithm for underperforming families
Export baseline for commercial review

topics.title: Commercial Overlay
topics.description: The commercial team reviews the statistical baseline and applies adjustments based on market intelligence the model cannot see — promotions, pipeline changes, new distribution wins, or lost accounts.
outputs: Assumption log (one entry per material override)
tasks: Distribute baseline forecast to commercial team for review
Collect commercial override proposals with documented assumptions
Validate overrides are within governance thresholds
Apply approved overrides in planning software
Produce assumption log for audit trail

topics.title: Consensus Forecast Review
topics.description: Commercial and supply chain align on a single consensus number. Disagreements are surfaced and resolved in the meeting — not after it. The output is one agreed forecast, not a range or multiple versions.
outputs: Meeting minutes with decisions and owners
tasks: Circulate pre-meeting pack 48 hours before review
Review actuals vs. prior forecast and document bias
Present statistical baseline and commercial overlays
Surface and resolve material disagreements in the meeting
Formally lock consensus forecast in planning software

topics.title: Forecast Lock & Distribution
topics.description: The agreed consensus forecast is locked in the system and distributed to downstream planning processes. Any post-lock revision requires a formal change request with an owner and documented reason.
tasks: Lock forecast in planning software for the agreed horizon
Trigger automated distribution to supply and inventory planning
Notify finance of final demand plan for reconciliation
Publish change request procedure to stakeholders
Archive assumption log and meeting minutes

topics.title: Capacity Assessment
topics.description: Assess available production and procurement capacity for the planning horizon against the locked demand forecast. Identify bottlenecks and headroom before running the constrained plan.
tasks: Load current capacity data into planning software
Run unconstrained supply run against consensus demand
Identify resources at or above 85% utilisation
Flag supplier capacity limits from latest confirmations
Produce capacity utilisation summary for review

topics.title: Constrained Supply Run
topics.description: Run the constrained supply plan in planning software to generate a feasible production and procurement schedule. The system applies prioritisation rules to allocate constrained capacity to demand.
tasks: Confirm prioritisation rules are current and approved
Trigger constrained supply run in planning software
Review run log for errors or constraint violations
Extract unmet demand report by product and time bucket
Validate constrained plan totals against unconstrained baseline

topics.title: Gap Identification
topics.description: Identify where the constrained supply plan cannot meet the consensus demand plan. Gaps are quantified by volume, value, and timing and classified by root cause to guide option development.
tasks: Compare constrained plan against demand forecast by family and time bucket
Calculate gap volume and value for each shortfall
Classify each gap (capacity, material, lead time, or policy constrained)
Identify gaps closable within operations authority
Flag gaps requiring cross-functional decision for pre-S&OP

topics.title: Option Development
topics.description: For each significant gap, develop costed options that could close it. The supply review should never present a gap without a proposed solution — even if none of the options are ideal.
tasks: Engage suppliers on flexibility for critical material gaps
Cost overtime and additional shift scenarios for capacity gaps
Evaluate subcontracting options where available
Model expedited logistics scenarios for timing-constrained gaps
Compile options with cost and lead time for each gap

topics.title: Supply Plan Submission
topics.description: Finalise and submit the supply plan for the pre-S&OP review, accompanied by the gap report and options list so the pre-S&OP team can make informed decisions.
tasks: Review and sign off constrained supply plan
Prepare gap and options summary for pre-S&OP
Load supply plan into planning software for distribution
Distribute pre-S&OP briefing pack to participants
Archive supply plan version for audit trail

topics.title: Coverage Analysis
topics.description: Assess current inventory positions against projected demand. Identify where coverage is below target, where excess stock is building, and where write-off risk exists.
outputs: Coverage report by product (weeks of supply)
tasks: Extract current stock positions from ERP
Calculate projected coverage in weeks by product family
Flag items below minimum coverage threshold
Flag items with excess stock above maximum threshold
Prepare coverage report for S&OP review

topics.title: Safety Stock Review
topics.description: Review and update safety stock targets to reflect current demand variability, supplier lead time variability, and agreed service level targets. Targets should be challenged periodically — not left static.
outputs: Updated safety stock targets by SKU
Exception list for items requiring policy discussion
tasks: Calculate demand variability (standard deviation) for each SKU over 13 weeks
Calculate lead time variability by supplier
Apply service level formula to derive safety stock quantities
Compare current targets against calculated values
Escalate material changes to S&OP process owner for approval

topics.title: Inventory Target Setting
topics.description: Set or confirm cycle stock and safety stock targets for the planning horizon. Targets balance working capital efficiency against service level obligations.
tasks: Enter updated safety stock targets in planning software
Run projected inventory value calculation for horizon
Compare projected value against working capital budget
Identify trade-offs where budget and service level targets conflict
Present trade-offs to S&OP process owner for decision

topics.title: Exception Resolution
topics.description: Resolve inventory exceptions flagged during coverage and target-setting — shortage risks requiring expediting, excess positions requiring action, and write-off candidates requiring sign-off.
tasks: Review and prioritise shortage risks by revenue impact
Raise expedite requests for critical shortages with supply planning
Propose redistribution or markdown plan for excess stock
Prepare write-off requests for finance approval
Update inventory plan with agreed actions

topics.title: Resource Demand Projection
topics.description: Translate the supply plan into resource requirements — labour, equipment, and warehouse capacity — across the planning horizon. This projection forms the basis for capacity mapping and constraint identification.
outputs: Resource demand projection by resource type
tasks: Run resource requirements projection in planning software
Extract load profile by resource type for the horizon
Review projection against known calendar exceptions (holidays, shutdowns)
Flag resources projected to exceed available capacity
Distribute projection to resource owners for validation

topics.title: Capacity Mapping
topics.description: Compare projected resource demand against available capacity to identify surplus and deficit positions. Available capacity accounts for planned maintenance, holidays, and contractual limits.
outputs: Capacity vs. demand map by resource
Surplus and deficit summary by period
tasks: Confirm resource capacity calendars are up to date
Run capacity vs. demand comparison in planning software
Produce heat map showing surplus and deficit by period
Validate results with resource owners
Update planning software with any corrections to capacity data

topics.title: Constraint Identification
topics.description: Identify which resource constraints are binding — those that will prevent the supply plan from being executed as planned. Not all constraints require action; only those that create plan risk.
tasks: Filter capacity map to resources at or above 100% utilisation
Assess whether binding constraints affect prioritised demand
Rank constraints by service level impact
Identify constraints resolvable within operations vs. those requiring escalation
Prepare constraint summary for pre-S&OP

topics.title: Resource Allocation
topics.description: Allocate available resource capacity to the supply plan, applying agreed prioritisation rules. Where constraints cannot be resolved, document the trade-off and escalate to pre-S&OP.
tasks: Apply prioritisation rules to allocate constrained capacity in planning software
Confirm resource-allocated plan is consistent with constrained supply plan
Document trade-offs where prioritisation results in unmet demand
Raise escalation items for unresolved constraints
Submit resource-allocated plan to supply planner for pre-S&OP integration

topics.title: Pre-S&OP Alignment
topics.description: The pre-S&OP meeting brings together the outputs of demand, supply, inventory, and resource planning to identify cross-functional issues that cannot be resolved within individual functions. The goal is to prepare a small number of clear decisions for executive review.
inputs: Supply plan with gap and options list
outputs: Issue and decision list for executive review
tasks: Distribute pre-S&OP briefing pack to participants 48 hours in advance
Review demand plan and supply plan alignment
Identify issues that cannot be resolved at functional level
Agree recommended options for each issue
Prepare executive S&OP pack with issues, options, and recommendations

topics.title: Financial Reconciliation
topics.description: Reconcile the volume plan with the financial plan to ensure consistency. Significant volume-to-value gaps must be explained and, where material, resolved before executive review.
tasks: Map volume plan to financial values using current price and cost assumptions
Compare resulting financial forecast against the approved financial plan
Calculate the bridge (volume, price, mix, and cost variances)
Identify items where the gap requires a management decision
Include financial summary in executive S&OP pack

topics.title: Executive S&OP Review
topics.description: The executive review is the decision-making forum of the S&OP cycle. It reviews the aligned plan, considers the issues and options prepared by pre-S&OP, and makes the decisions needed to produce one agreed business plan.
outputs: Action log with owners and due dates
tasks: Present prior period performance vs. plan
Review horizon demand and supply plan with financial overlay
Present issues with options and pre-S&OP recommendations
Facilitate executive decisions on escalated items
Record decisions and assign actions with owners and due dates

topics.title: Plan Distribution & Close
topics.description: The agreed decisions from the executive review are applied to the plan in planning software. The final plan is distributed to all functions as the authoritative business plan. The cycle is formally closed and the next cycle is initiated.
outputs: Approved final plan (distributed to all functions)
tasks: Apply executive decisions to plan in planning software
Lock and publish the approved plan
Distribute plan to all functions with a summary of key decisions
Circulate action tracker with owners and due dates
Schedule and confirm next S&OP cycle start date

topics.title: The Weekly S&OE Cadence
topics.description: A structured weekly cadence gives the S&OE process rhythm and discipline — ensuring the right reviews happen in the right order before the week's plans are committed.

topics.title: S&OE Roles and Participants
topics.description: S&OE is an operational process driven by planners — with limited executive involvement. Understanding who owns what in the weekly cycle prevents bottlenecks and misaligned escalation.

topics.title: Escalating from S&OE to S&OP
topics.description: S&OE resolves operational deviations within the monthly plan. When a deviation is too large or persistent to manage tactically, it must be escalated to the S&OP cycle.

topics.title: Monitoring the Near-Term Plan
topics.description: S&OE starts with visibility — knowing where the current week's plan stands before deciding where to intervene.

topics.title: Making Adjustments and Resetting the Plan
topics.description: When exceptions cannot be auto-resolved, planners intervene — adjusting orders, schedules, and allocations to keep the near-term plan feasible.

topics.title: The Integrated S&OE Review
topics.description: The weekly integrated review aligns demand and supply on the near-term plan — resolving cross-functional conflicts and finalising the escalation list for S&OP.

topics.title: Handoff to Execution
topics.description: The S&OE cycle ends when the confirmed near-term plan is published to execution teams — making the transition from planning to action clean and unambiguous.

topics.title: What to Monitor
topics.description: The demand signals an S&OE planner watches each week and why each one matters.

topics.title: Monitoring in Practice
topics.description: How demand monitoring works inside planning software — workspaces, exception queues, and the actions available per exception type.

topics.title: Escalation from Demand Monitoring
topics.description: When demand deviations exceed what S&OE can absorb — the thresholds, decision logic, and communication steps for escalating to S&OP.

topics.title: Supply Signals
topics.description: The supply signals an S&OE planner tracks each week and how each feeds the monitoring picture.

topics.title: Supply Exceptions and Response
topics.description: How supply exceptions surface in planning software, what responses are available, and when the planner acts versus escalates.

topics.title: Supplier Performance Tracking
topics.description: Monitoring supplier performance as part of S&OE supply monitoring and when persistent underperformance escalates to S&OP.

topics.title: Purpose and Structure
topics.description: What the integrated S&OE review is, how it differs from S&OP, and who participates.

topics.title: The Review Agenda
topics.description: The standard five-part S&OE integrated review agenda and how to run a tight 60-minute meeting.

topics.title: Decisions and Outputs
topics.description: What the integrated S&OE review produces and how its outputs flow to execution teams and into the S&OP cycle.

topics.title: What Is Execution?
topics.description: Execution is the operational layer where plans become actions — orders are picked, production runs, deliveries are made. It operates within hours and days, not weeks and months.

topics.title: From Plan to Action
topics.description: How the S&OE-confirmed plan becomes operational instructions — the technical and process steps that translate planning outputs into execution inputs.

topics.title: Managing Execution Exceptions
topics.description: Execution rarely runs exactly to plan. Real-time exceptions — machine breakdowns, missing stock, transport failures — require fast, structured responses to protect service level.

topics.title: Execution and ERP
topics.description: ERP is the system of record for execution. Understanding how planning software and ERP interact — and where each one's authority begins and ends — is essential for clean execution.

topics.title: Why Prioritisation Is a Policy
topics.description: Order prioritisation must be governed by policy, not left to individual planner judgment — and the planning system is where that policy is enforced.

topics.title: Prioritisation Criteria
topics.description: The standard criteria used to rank orders when supply is constrained, and how those criteria stack when two high-priority orders compete.

topics.title: Allocation Policy and Escalation
topics.description: How allocation policy in planning software automates prioritisation, when execution must escalate, and what a good escalation to S&OE looks like.

topics.title: What Execution Monitoring Covers
topics.description: What execution monitoring means, what it tracks, and how it is distinct from planning.

topics.title: Real-Time Visibility in Practice
topics.description: How real-time visibility works across goods receipts, production, warehouse, and carrier feeds — and why inventory record accuracy is the foundation everything else depends on.

topics.title: End-of-Day Reconciliation
topics.description: The end-of-period reconciliation discipline: what it covers, how it is done, and why it is the starting point for the S&OE planner's next day.

topics.title: What Actuals Capture Means
topics.description: What actuals capture is, why it must happen at the point of the event, and why it is the foundation the entire planning cycle depends on.

topics.title: Recording Discipline by Transaction Type
topics.description: The four main execution transaction types, what correct recording looks like for each, and the cost of recording them late or inaccurately.

topics.title: Reason Codes and Continuous Improvement
topics.description: Why reason codes on execution deviations are the feedback mechanism that allows S&OE and S&OP to identify patterns and improve over time.

topics.title: The Feedback Loop
topics.description: Why the feedback loop from execution to planning is not optional, and the three data flows that close it.

topics.title: Integration Lag and Its Implications
topics.description: The time lag between a physical execution event and its appearance in the planning model — and what it means for how planners should interpret exceptions during the day.

topics.title: Systematic Variances and Escalation
topics.description: The difference between one-off execution variances and systematic ones — and when a variance must be escalated to S&OE or S&OP rather than absorbed as an operational adjustment.

topics.title: Service Level Targets
topics.description: Service level targets define how reliably the organisation commits to fulfilling customer demand. They are a policy decision that balances customer satisfaction against inventory cost.

topics.title: Safety Stock Policy
topics.description: Safety stock is the buffer inventory held to absorb demand and supply variability. The policy determines how much to hold, where to hold it, and when to replenish it.

topics.title: Allocation Policy
topics.description: When supply cannot meet all demand, allocation policy determines which customers get which quantities. Defining this in advance prevents ad hoc decisions under pressure and ensures commercial priorities are respected.

topics.title: Prioritization Frameworks
topics.description: Prioritization determines which orders, products, and customers are served first when resources are constrained. A clear framework prevents conflict and ensures the planning system reflects actual business strategy.

topics.title: Decision Framework
topics.description: A decision framework defines which planning decisions are made by whom, at what level, and within what authority. It prevents confusion, reduces escalation bottlenecks, and ensures accountability is clear.

topics.title: Escalation Paths
topics.description: Escalation paths define how planning issues move from the team that identifies them to the level of authority needed to resolve them. Clear paths prevent issues from stalling and ensure decisions happen in time to matter.

topics.title: KPI Framework Overview
topics.description: A KPI framework for supply chain planning covers four domains: service, inventory, efficiency, and forecast accuracy. Understanding the structure prevents the common trap of measuring everything while managing nothing.

topics.title: Key Planning KPIs Defined
topics.description: Precise definitions for the most important supply chain planning KPIs — what each metric measures, how it is calculated, and what a good result looks like.

topics.title: KPI Ownership and Review Cadence
topics.description: KPIs only drive behaviour if someone owns them and they are reviewed regularly. Assigning ownership and embedding KPI review into the planning cadence transforms metrics from reports into management tools.

topics.title: KPIs and Exception Management
topics.description: KPIs and exception-based working are two sides of the same management system. KPIs measure outcomes; exceptions drive the daily actions that determine those outcomes.

topics.title: Execution KPIs
topics.description: Execution KPIs measure what actually happened — they are the feedback signal and early warning system that connects daily operations back to the planning cycle.

topics.title: What is Scenario Planning?
topics.description: Scenario planning is the discipline of exploring alternative futures before committing to a course of action. In Planning software, it means running parallel plans.

topics.title: When to Use Scenario Planning
topics.description: Not every planning decision warrants a scenario. Knowing the triggers — demand uncertainty, supply risk, new launches — helps you apply the practice where it adds the most value.

topics.title: Types of Scenarios in Supply Chain
topics.description: Demand scenarios, supply scenarios, and constraint scenarios serve different questions. Understanding the types helps you frame the right what-if.

topics.title: The Scenario Planning Process
topics.description: A good scenario planning process has four steps: define the question, model the assumption, compare the outputs, and decide. Skipping steps creates confusion.

topics.title: Setting Up Effective Scenarios
topics.description: The quality of a scenario depends on how it is framed. Good scenarios are specific, comparable, and bounded.

topics.title: Scenarios for Supply Risks
topics.description: Supply-side disruptions — supplier failures, capacity shortfalls, logistics delays — require specific scenario types that model constrained supply and test your planning resilience.

topics.title: Scenarios for Demand Uncertainty
topics.description: Demand uncertainty — upside spikes, downside misses, customer concentration risk — requires scenarios that stress-test your supply plan against plausible demand alternatives.

topics.title: Creating a Scenario in Planning software
topics.description: A scenario in Planning software starts as a copy of the baseline plan. This topic covers how to create one, name it, and scope it correctly before making any changes.

topics.title: Adjusting Parameters & Assumptions
topics.description: The substance of a scenario is the change you make to the model. This topic covers the main levers — demand, supply, capacity, and time — and how to adjust them precisely.

topics.title: Running & Comparing Scenarios
topics.description: After making adjustments, you run the planning engine inside the scenario and compare the results against the baseline. This is where the value of scenario planning becomes visible.

topics.title: Promoting a Scenario to Baseline
topics.description: When a scenario represents the plan the business wants to execute, it can be promoted to become the new baseline. This is a consequential action that requires care.

topics.title: Communicating Scenario Findings
topics.description: A scenario that produces good analysis but isn't communicated well doesn't drive good decisions. This topic covers how to translate scenario outputs into stakeholder conversations.

topics.title: What Is Exception Management
topics.description: The discipline of reviewing only deviations from plan beyond acceptable bounds — and trusting that in-tolerance items do not need intervention.

topics.title: Exception Types
topics.description: The standard exception categories across S&OP, S&OE, and Execution — what each signals, what triggers it, and who owns it.

topics.title: Exception Resolution
topics.description: The workflow for investigating and resolving exceptions — from queue triage to root cause to escalation decision.

topics.title: Calibrating Exception Thresholds
topics.description: Why exception thresholds require ongoing calibration — and how to tune them so the queue stays actionable without missing real problems.

topics.title: What Is a Constraint
topics.description: What constraints are in supply chain planning, how they arise, and how they are represented in the planning model.

topics.title: Identifying Binding Constraints
topics.description: How to distinguish a binding constraint from a non-binding one, and how planning software surfaces constraint information during supply review.

topics.title: Managing Constraints in the Planning Cycle
topics.description: The three constraint management strategies and how constraint decisions flow through S&OE, S&OP, and strategic planning.

topics.title: What is Data-Driven Planning?
topics.description: A planning process is only as good as the data it runs on. This topic defines what data-driven planning means in practice.

topics.title: How Data Flows into a Plan
topics.description: From raw data load to a calculated plan — the sequence of how Planning software processes incoming data and translates it into planning outputs.

topics.title: The Cost of Bad Data
topics.description: Bad planning data doesn't stay in the system — it becomes inventory, stockouts, and wasted capacity. Understanding the downstream effects makes data quality feel urgent.

topics.title: What Is Master Data?
topics.description: Master data is the stable, reference information that describes the objects in the planning model — items, locations, customers, suppliers. It changes slowly but when it's wrong, everything built on it is wrong.

topics.title: What Is Transactional Data?
topics.description: Transactional data records what has happened: inventory movements, order receipts, production completions. It is the evidence of execution — and the foundation for keeping the plan grounded in reality.

topics.title: The Planning software Data Model Overview
topics.description: Planning software represents the supply chain as a graph of connected nodes. Understanding this structure is the foundation for understanding how data works.

topics.title: Where Data Comes From
topics.description: Planning data doesn't originate in Planning software — it is loaded from source systems. Understanding the sources helps you understand why data problems occur.

topics.title: What Makes Data "Good Enough"?
topics.description: Data quality isn't binary. Four dimensions — completeness, accuracy, timeliness, and consistency — define whether data is fit for planning.

topics.title: Common Data Problems in Planning
topics.description: The same data problems appear in almost every supply chain planning implementation. Recognising them is the first step to fixing them.

topics.title: How Data Quality Shows Up in Plans
topics.description: Data problems have recognisable signatures in planning outputs. Learning to read the plan as a diagnostic tool helps you surface data issues before they become operational problems.

topics.title: Improving Data Quality Over Time
topics.description: Data quality is not a one-time clean-up — it is an ongoing capability. This topic introduces the practices that sustain planning data quality as the business evolves.

topics.title: Data Confidence
topics.description: Data confidence is the degree of trust that planners place in the data they are working with. Low-confidence data undermines decisions; understanding where confidence is low is the first step to improving it.

topics.title: Data Ownership
topics.description: Every data element needs an owner — someone accountable for its accuracy, completeness, and currency. Without clear ownership, data quality degrades and no one is responsible for fixing it.

topics.title: Data Definitions & Single Source of Truth
topics.description: When the same concept means different things in different systems or to different people, planning decisions get made on inconsistent data. Clear definitions and a single source of truth are the foundation of data reliability.

topics.title: Data Governance Basics
topics.description: Data quality doesn't maintain itself. Governance defines who is responsible for which data, how changes are approved, and how issues are escalated.

topics.title: The Four Systems
topics.description: What ERP, Planning Software, MDM system, and FMS each do — and why the planning landscape needs all four.

topics.title: How the Landscape Fits Together
topics.description: How ERP, Planning Software, MDM system, and FMS connect — what flows between them, when, and what breaks when it doesn't.

topics.title: What This Means for Planners
topics.description: How to work effectively in a multi-system landscape — tracing numbers to their source, understanding data latency, and fixing problems in the right place.

topics.title: System of Record
topics.description: What 'system of record' means, which systems own which data types in the planning landscape, and what happens when that discipline breaks down.

topics.title: System of Engagement
topics.description: What 'system of engagement' means, which systems planners and field teams work in daily, and why the SoE depends entirely on the health of the systems feeding it.

topics.title: System of Intelligence
topics.description: How Planning Software generates exceptions and recommendations, why SoI outputs are only as good as their inputs, and how to diagnose when the intelligence layer gets it wrong.

topics.title: System Interfaces Overview
topics.description: The four systems in the landscape — ERP, Planning software, FMS, and MDM system — are connected by a set of defined interfaces. Understanding what flows where is the foundation for troubleshooting and governance.

topics.title: Batch Runs
topics.description: Batch runs are the scheduled jobs that keep the planning model current — refreshing transactional data, triggering planning engine recalculations, and synchronising master data.

topics.title: Data Upload
topics.description: Not all data flows through automated interfaces. Manual data uploads handle one-off corrections, new data sources, and situations where the automated interface is not yet available.

topics.title: ERP ↔ Planning software Integration
topics.description: How the ERP ↔ Planning software link works technically — what triggers the exchange, the batch cycle that drives it, common failure modes, and the governance control that prevents unreviewed planned orders from auto-releasing to ERP.

topics.title: Planning software ↔ FMS & MDM system
topics.description: How Planning software connects to FMS and MDM technically — integration frequency, the master data dependency chain that must complete before a new item is plannable, and why item-code mapping between systems is a recurring failure point.

topics.title: The Supply Planner's Tool Interactions
topics.description: How a supply planner uses Planning Software, ERP, MDM system, and FMS — what they look for, what they act on, and how they use each system to check the others.

topics.title: The Demand Planner's Tool Interactions
topics.description: How a demand planner uses Planning Software, ERP, MDM system, and FMS — building forecasts, applying overlays, calibrating against actuals, and understanding supply constraints.

topics.title: The Data Manager's Tool Interactions
topics.description: How a data manager works across MDM system, ERP, Planning Software, and FMS — maintaining master data, validating downstream propagation, and tracing data quality problems to their source.

topics.title: Item
topics.description: The products, materials, and SKUs that flow through your supply chain network.
topics.summary: An Item is the most fundamental building block in Planning software — anything that can be planned, produced, stored, bought, or sold. It can be a raw material, a semi-finished good, a finished product, or a packaging component. Every supply and demand signal, every production order, and every purchase requisition in Planning software is tied to an item. Think of items as the nouns of your supply chain: everything else acts on them or connects them.
bullets: Has a unique Item ID across the entire network
Classified as raw material, WIP, or finished good
Carries unit of measure, shelf life, and planning horizon
Can be stocked at multiple network locations
Every supply and demand signal in Planning software is tied to an item

topics.title: Item @ Location
topics.description: How an item is represented at a specific node in the network, and what that means for planning.
topics.summary: An Item @ Location is the planning unit in Planning software — the combination of a specific item stocked at a specific node in the network. Every inventory balance, safety stock policy, and supply or demand signal exists at this level. The same physical product can behave differently at different locations: one node might hold safety stock, another might operate make-to-order. Planning software plans each item-location pair independently, then reconciles them across the network.
bullets: Represents a specific item stocked at a specific network node
The item + location pair is the fundamental planning unit
Each location can have its own stock policy — min, max, safety stock
Inventory balances and on-hand quantities are tracked at this level
Planning software generates supply and demand signals per item-location combination

topics.title: BOM (Bill of Material)
topics.description: The Bill of Materials — how input items are consumed and output items are produced in a transformation process.
topics.summary: A Transformation Process is the recipe that defines how items are made. It specifies which input items are consumed, which output items are produced, the yield per unit, the lead time, and the facility where it happens. In Planning software this is known as the Bill of Materials (BOM) — and unlike traditional ERP, BOM and routing are unified into a single object. Processes can be chained: the output of one becomes the input of another, allowing Planning software to resolve multi-level structures automatically when generating supply plans.
bullets: Consumes one or more input items, produces one or more outputs
Defines yield: how much output per unit of input consumed
Has a lead time — the duration of the transformation
Runs at a specific location (plant or manufacturing node)
Can be chained to form multi-level Bills of Materials

topics.title: BOM: Input & Output
topics.description: What goes into a transformation process, what comes out, and the yield that connects the two.
topics.summary: Every transformation process has two sides: what goes in and what comes out. The input side (consumed items) defines which raw materials or components are used and in what quantity. The output side (produced items) defines what is created and at what yield. Yield captures the efficiency gap — if you feed 100 units of input into a process and only 80 units of usable output emerge, the yield is 80%. That 20% loss is not a mistake in the model; it reflects real-world scrap, evaporation, trimming, or conversion losses. Planning software uses yield when exploding demand backwards: to produce 800 finished units at 80% yield, the plan must schedule enough input to cover 1,000 units of consumption.
bullets: Consumed items define which inputs are used and at what quantity per output
Produced items define which outputs are created when the process runs
Yield captures the efficiency gap between input consumed and output produced
A yield of 80% means 100 units in → 80 usable units out
Planning software divides required output by yield to calculate gross input requirements

topics.title: BOM: Lead time
topics.description: The duration of a transformation process — from when it starts to when the output item is available.
topics.summary: BOM Lead Time is the total duration of a transformation process — the time from when production starts to when the output item is available for use or shipment. The planning engine uses lead time to offset supply signals backwards from a due date: if a product is needed on day 10 and takes 3 days to make, production must start on day 7. Accurate lead times are critical for generating feasible plans that don't promise what can't be delivered.
bullets: Total duration from process start to output item availability
Drives backward scheduling — when Planning software must start production
Typically expressed in hours or days depending on the process
Can include queue time, setup time, and run time components
Longer lead times require earlier supply signals to avoid stockouts

topics.title: BOD (Bill of Distribution)
topics.description: The Bill of Distribution — how items move between locations and what constraints govern each transport lane.
topics.summary: A Transportation Process defines how an item moves from one location to another — without changing its form or identity. This is the Bill of Distribution (BOD) in Planning software, and it defines origin, destination, transit time, and shipment constraints. When demand exists at a downstream node, Planning software traces back through transportation lanes to find the upstream source. Unlike a transformation process, which happens at a node and changes an item, a transportation process happens between nodes and simply relocates it.
bullets: Moves an item without changing its form or identity
Defines origin node, destination node, and allowed item(s)
Carries transit time, min/max order quantity, and transport mode
Generates transfer orders or shipments in the execution system
Sourcing rules can prioritize lanes — e.g. nearest DC first

topics.title: BOD: Transport mode
topics.description: The method of transport on a lane — truck, rail, sea, air — and how it affects cost and lead time.
topics.summary: Transport Mode defines the physical method of movement on a distribution lane — truck, rail, sea, or air. Each mode carries a different lead time, cost profile, and capacity constraint. The planning engine uses transport mode to determine which lanes are feasible for a given replenishment need and when shipments must depart to arrive on time. Multiple modes can be defined on a single lane, allowing Planning software to choose the best option based on urgency and cost.
bullets: Defines the physical method of movement on a lane — truck, rail, sea, air
Each mode has a different lead time, cost, and capacity profile
Affects replenishment cycle frequency and planning horizon
Multiple modes can be defined on a single origin-destination lane
Mode selection balances service level against transportation cost

topics.title: BOD: Transport lead time
topics.description: The time an item spends in transit on a distribution lane, from departure at origin to arrival at destination.
topics.summary: Transport Lead Time is the time an item spends in transit on a distribution lane — from the moment it departs the origin node to the moment it arrives at the destination node. The planning engine uses transit time to offset shipment release dates: if a DC needs stock on day 10 and transit takes 3 days, the shipment must leave the warehouse on day 7. Accurate transit times prevent phantom supply — situations where the plan assumes goods are available before they actually arrive.
bullets: Time from departure at origin to arrival at destination
Drives shipment release dates — when Planning software must trigger a transfer order
Expressed in days or hours depending on the distribution network
Longer transit times increase in-transit (pipeline) inventory
Varies by transport mode — air is faster, sea and road are slower

topics.title: Resource
topics.description: The machines, lines, and people whose capacity constrains how much you can produce.
topics.summary: A Resource is anything with limited capacity that gets consumed during a transformation process — a machine, a production line, a crew, or a tank. Resources are what turn an unconstrained supply explosion into a feasible, real-world plan. A resource's availability is governed by a calendar that captures shifts, planned maintenance, and holidays. When Planning software checks feasibility, it uses available capacity — not theoretical maximum — to determine whether the plan can actually be executed.
bullets: Has available capacity per time bucket (hours or units)
Governed by a calendar: shifts, maintenance, and holidays
Types: machine, labor, storage, or utility
Belongs to a specific location in the network
Consumed by transformation processes via resource consumptions

topics.title: Resource Consumed
topics.description: The link between transformation processes and resources — how much capacity each process uses.
topics.summary: Resource Consumed is the link that connects a transformation process to a resource — it specifies how much capacity is used each time the process runs. Without this link, the planning engine has no way to know that making 1,000 units of Product X will occupy Bottling Line 3 for 8 hours. Consumption can be fixed (a setup cost paid once per batch) or variable (a run rate proportional to output). Planning software sums all resource consumptions across the plan and compares them against available capacity to identify bottlenecks.
bullets: Links a transformation process to the resource it consumes
Defines the consumption rate — capacity used per unit of output
Fixed consumption covers setup time; variable covers run rate
Summed across all planned batches to check resource feasibility
Bottleneck resources constrain the total achievable supply plan

topics.title: The Starting Items
topics.description: Item A and Item B are the upstream seed inputs that start the entire supply chain.

topics.title: Shipping Items to the Grower
topics.description: Two parallel BOD lanes move Item A and Item B from the Central Warehouse to the Grower.

topics.title: Seed Production Node
topics.description: A BOM process at the Grower consumes Item A and Item B to produce Item C.

topics.title: Shipping Seeds Back
topics.description: Item C is transported from the Grower back to the Central Warehouse via a BOD lane.

topics.title: Cleaning Node
topics.description: Item C is cleaned at the Central Warehouse to produce Item D.

topics.title: Calibrating Node
topics.description: Calibrating splits Item D into three co-products: Items E, F, and G.

topics.title: Priming Node
topics.description: Item F is primed to improve germination, producing Item H.

topics.title: Coating Node
topics.description: Primed seeds (Item H) receive a polymer coating to produce Item I.

topics.title: Packing Node
topics.description: Coated seeds (Item I) are packed into saleable units to produce Item J.

topics.title: Final Shipment Node
topics.description: Item J is dispatched to the customer via a BOD lane, completing the network.

topics.title: The Complete Network
topics.description: The complete supply chain network as a graph — all nodes, edges, and the planning model that connects them.

topics.title: What Is a Demand Signal?
topics.description: Demand in S&OP is not a physical flow — it is an information signal that travels upstream through the network to trigger planning.

topics.title: Where Demand Enters the Network
topics.description: The customer forecast lands on Item J — the finished packed seed — and this single number sets the entire upstream plan in motion.

topics.title: Demand Propagating Upstream
topics.description: Watch the 100-unit forecast travel step by step from Item J back to the raw material inputs Items A and B.

topics.title: Lead Times and Demand Netting
topics.description: How process lead times shift demand backward in time, and how on-hand inventory reduces the quantity that must be planned.

topics.title: What Is a Supply Signal?
topics.description: Once demand has been netted and time-phased, the planning engine generates supply responses — planned orders that move downstream through the network.

topics.title: Inventory Netting
topics.description: On-hand inventory is the first source of supply. The planning engine nets available stock before triggering any new planned order.

topics.title: Parent Line Items
topics.description: Every item in the network has a parent — the upstream source that must supply it. Understanding this hierarchy is the key to reading supply plans.

topics.title: Supply Flowing Downstream
topics.description: Watch planned orders and inventory flow step by step from Items A and B all the way to the customer.

topics.title: Disaggregation: Variety to Item
topics.description: How a high-level variety forecast is broken down into individual item-level demand signals.

topics.title: Disaggregation: Year to Month
topics.description: How an annual plan is distributed across months using seasonal profiles and planning rules.

topics.title: How Backward Consumption Works
topics.description: How firm orders consume forecast quantities, preventing double-counting of demand in the planning horizon.

topics.title: What Is a Scheduled Receipt?
topics.description: An existing supply order that is already in progress and will arrive at a known future date.

topics.title: Push Planning Logic
topics.description: A planning strategy where supply is generated based on forecast, ahead of actual demand.

topics.title: Pull Planning Logic
topics.description: A planning strategy where supply is triggered only by actual downstream demand or consumption.

topics.title: How Safety Stock Is Calculated
topics.description: A buffer of inventory held above the cycle stock to protect against demand variability and supply uncertainty.

topics.title: How Demand Slicing Works
topics.description: How splitting demand into equal micro-buckets creates a fair, rotation-based inventory allocation when supply is constrained.

topics.title: What Is a Workflow Simulation?
topics.description: A workflow simulation in Planning software is an end-to-end what-if run that lets you change assumptions, recalculate the plan, and compare the result against the baseline — without touching the live plan.

topics.title: Setting Up a Simulation
topics.description: Before running a simulation in Planning software, you need to define what you are testing, create the scenario, and make the right input changes. Preparation determines whether the output is useful.

topics.title: Running the Simulation
topics.description: Triggering the Planning software planning engine recalculates the supply plan end-to-end. Understanding what happens during the run helps you interpret the output correctly.

topics.title: Reading Simulation Results
topics.description: The value of a simulation is in how you read and compare its output. Knowing where to look and what to compare helps you extract a clear answer from the recalculated plan.

topics.title: Promoting a Simulation to Baseline
topics.description: Promotion is the step where a scenario becomes the new plan. It is the most consequential action in the simulation workflow and requires the right governance before it is taken.

topics.title: Navigating Planning software
topics.description: Planning software is organised around a hierarchy of workspaces, views, and tabs. Understanding the navigation structure helps you move efficiently between planning tasks without losing context.

topics.title: Planning Software Interface Overview
topics.description: The Planning software interface is built around a grid-and-chart model. Understanding the core UI components — grids, filters, charts, and the exception panel — makes every workspace easier to use.

topics.title: Reading Plan Output
topics.description: Plan output is what the Planning software planning engine produces: planned orders, inventory projections, exception flags. Knowing how to read these outputs is the core skill of working in Planning software.

topics.title: Navigating the Demand Workspace
topics.description: How to open the Demand workspace, apply your location filter, and work through the grid.
steps: Open the Demand workspace from the left menu
Set the Location filter to your site
Expand a product family row to see SKU-level data
Review any red cells — these are exceptions
Save the view with your filter settings

topics.title: Standard Prose Layout
topics.description: The default reading layout — markdown content rendered as structured prose with chapter-coloured headings and pull quotes.

topics.title: Card Grid Layout
topics.description: Use this layout to present a collection of concepts, modules, or features as a scannable grid of cards.

cards.title: Demand Planning
cards.description: Statistical and machine-learning forecasts that drive the upstream supply plan.

cards.title: Supply Planning
cards.description: Constraint-aware supply generation across the full bill of materials and distribution network.

cards.title: Inventory Optimisation
cards.description: Safety stock and reorder point logic that balances service levels against working capital.

cards.title: Production Scheduling
cards.description: Detailed finite-capacity scheduling that sequences work orders across machines and shifts.

cards.title: S&OP
cards.description: Monthly cross-functional alignment of volume and financial plans at an aggregate level.

cards.title: Scenario Planning
cards.description: Side-by-side what-if comparison of plan variants before a decision is locked in.

topics.title: Data Table Layout
topics.description: Reference data presented as a clean, scrollable table — ideal for comparisons, specs, and field listings.
tableRows: Item ID, UOM, shelf life, planning horizon
Input items, output item, yield, lead time, location
Origin, destination, transit time, item, mode
Item, quantity, due date, customer, priority
Item, quantity, start date, source, status

topics.title: Comparison Layout
topics.description: Two columns side-by-side — use this when you want to contrast two approaches, modes, or concepts directly.

left.title: Unconstrained Planning
points: Generates supply orders without checking capacity or material availability
Fast to compute — no constraint solving required
Useful as a first pass to see the raw volume of supply needed
Output often needs manual review and adjustment before execution
Common in long-horizon S&OP where operational detail doesn't matter yet

right.title: Constrained Planning
points: Respects capacity limits, lead times, MOQs, and material availability
Produces a feasible, executable plan the first time
Requires complete and accurate master data to run correctly
More compute-intensive — especially with complex multi-level BOMs
Used in short-to-medium horizon supply and production planning

topics.title: Full-Width Widget Layout
topics.description: The interactive simulation takes centre stage — prose context scrolls below the widget instead of above it.

topics.title: Node Topic Layout
topics.description: Used for the four core network objects — Items, Transformation Processes, Transportation Processes, and Resources.
topics.summary: The Node Topic layout is designed for concepts that have a canonical visual representation in the Planning software data model. The left panel shows an SVG diagram of how the object sits in the network graph; the right panel lists its key characteristics as bullet points. A summary card below ties it together with a plain-language description. Use this layout when you're introducing one of the four network primitives: Item, Transformation Process, Transportation Process, or Resource.
bullets: Consumes one or more input items, produces one or more outputs
Defines yield: how much output per unit of input consumed
Has a lead time — the duration of the transformation
Runs at a specific location (plant or manufacturing node)
Can be chained to form multi-level Bills of Materials

topics.title: Prose + Widget Layout
topics.description: The standard topic layout when a topic has both explanatory text and an interactive simulation — prose first, widget below.

topics.title: Getting Started with Configuration
topics.description: An orientation to the configuration layer of Planning software — what you configure, in what order, and the principles that make configuration changes safe and reversible.

topics.title: Configuring Items
topics.description: How to set up item records in Planning software — item codes, descriptions, units of measure, and the attributes that control how items behave in the plan.

topics.title: Configuring BOMs
topics.description: Step-by-step configuration of Bills of Material — inputs, outputs, lead times, and yield factors that define how supply transforms through the production network.

topics.title: Configuring BODs
topics.description: Step-by-step configuration of Bills of Distribution — lanes, transport modes, lead times, and the network paths that define how supply flows between locations.

topics.title: Configuring Resources
topics.description: How to set up resource records — capacities, calendars, and consumption rules that constrain what the plan can schedule.

topics.title: Planning Parameters
topics.description: Configuring safety stock levels, minimum order quantities, lot sizes, and the other parameters that govern how the planning engine generates recommendations.

topics.title: What Is an ERP?
topics.description: ERP is the system of record for all business transactions. Understanding what it does — and what it doesn't do — clarifies where planning ends and execution begins.

topics.title: ERP vs. legacy ERP
topics.description: ERP is the evolution of legacy ERP. Understanding what changed — and what stayed the same — sets the foundation for working effectively in the new ERP.

topics.title: Core ERP Concepts
topics.description: Items, locations, orders, postings, dimensions — the foundational concepts that appear throughout ERP and that every planner needs to understand.

topics.title: How ERP Structures Data
topics.description: ERP organises all business data around items, locations, and transactions. Understanding this structure explains why data looks the way it does when it arrives in Planning software.

topics.title: Key Master Data in ERP
topics.description: Item cards, vendor cards, locations, and units of measure — the master data records that every transaction in ERP references.

topics.title: Key Transactional Data in ERP
topics.description: Purchase orders, production orders, sales orders, and inventory transactions — the records that capture what is happening and what has happened.

topics.title: Where Data Originates
topics.description: ERP data comes from multiple sources: manual entry, EDI feeds, system integrations, and automated postings. Knowing the source explains the reliability of each data type.

topics.title: How Operational Data Enters ERP
topics.description: From purchase order confirmation to goods receipt to production order completion — how each operational event is captured in ERP.

topics.title: Data Validation and Posting
topics.description: ERP validates data before posting. Understanding what can prevent a transaction from posting — and what posting means — is essential for catching errors before they affect the plan.

topics.title: What ERP Sends to Planning
topics.description: Confirmed orders, inventory levels, and production actuals — the signals that flow from ERP into Planning software to keep the plan current.

topics.title: ERP Output to Other Systems
topics.description: Beyond Planning software, ERP sends data to finance, logistics, and reporting systems. Understanding the full output picture prevents duplication and reconciliation errors.

topics.title: Reconciling ERP and Planning Data
topics.description: When ERP and Planning software show different numbers, the difference always has a cause. How to identify timing gaps, integration delays, and data mismatches.

topics.title: ERP Business Rules
topics.description: ERP enforces rules on every transaction — posting groups, approval workflows, quantity constraints. Understanding these rules explains why some actions require specific setup.

topics.title: Order Management Logic
topics.description: How ERP handles the lifecycle of a purchase or production order — from creation to release to receipt or completion — and what each status means for the plan.

topics.title: Inventory Management Logic
topics.description: How ERP calculates on-hand, available, and projected inventory — including the difference between physical stock and what the planning system sees.

topics.title: Creating and Managing Orders
topics.description: How planners release planned orders from Planning software as purchase or production orders in ERP, and how to manage order changes through the execution cycle.

topics.title: Goods Receipt and Issue
topics.description: Receiving purchased goods and issuing materials to production — the two most frequent inventory transactions planners need to understand and monitor.

topics.title: Reporting and Extraction
topics.description: How to pull the data you need from ERP — standard reports, list exports, and the queries planners run to monitor order status and inventory position.

topics.title: Navigating ERP
topics.description: Effective ERP navigation means knowing when to use search, when to use the menu, and how to use filters and views to work efficiently with large data sets.

topics.title: The ERP Interface
topics.description: The ERP web client is organised around role centres, pages, and lists. Understanding the interface structure helps you navigate efficiently and find what you need.

topics.title: Reading ERP Output
topics.description: How to interpret the data ERP surfaces — understanding order statuses, inventory figures, and report output so you can act on what you see.

topics.title: Why FMS and MDM Are Supporting Systems
topics.description: FMS and MDM are grouped not because they are similar, but because neither manages transactions or planning calculations — they supply the reference data and field signals that make ERP and Planning software work correctly.

topics.title: FMS vs MDM: Key Differences
topics.description: FMS and MDM are often confused because both feed Planning software — but they manage entirely different data types and serve entirely different planning needs.

topics.title: How FMS and MDM Interact
topics.description: FMS and MDM are not directly integrated with each other — but they share a critical dependency: FMS uses item codes that must match the MDM master record exactly, or the integration to Planning software breaks.

topics.title: What Is an FMS?
topics.description: FMS tracks grower activity, contracts, and crop development. For supply chain planners, it is the primary source of supply-side signals for agricultural produce.

topics.title: Why Field Management Matters
topics.description: Agricultural supply plans are only as good as the field data that feeds them. Understanding why FMS exists explains the supply-side data quality issues planners encounter.

topics.title: Core FMS Concepts
topics.description: Grower contracts, planted area, yield forecasts, harvest schedules — the foundational concepts in FMS and what each one means for the supply plan.

topics.title: How FMS Structures Data
topics.description: FMS organises field data around growers, contracts, fields, and crop events. Understanding this structure explains how supply signals are generated and what they represent.

topics.title: Key Field Data Entities
topics.description: Growers, contracts, planted area, crop stages, and yield estimates — the core entities in FMS and what each one contributes to the supply plan.

topics.title: Field-to-Plan Data Mapping
topics.description: How field entities in FMS map to items, locations, and supply orders in Planning software — bridging the agronomic world and the planning model.

topics.title: Where Field Data Originates
topics.description: Field data does not start in a system — it starts in a field. Understanding where each type of field data comes from is the foundation for understanding what FMS can and cannot tell the planning model.

topics.title: How Field Activity Enters FMS
topics.description: Grower contracts, planting declarations, and crop progress updates each enter FMS through a different pathway — manual entry, mobile capture, or integration from agronomic tools.

topics.title: Data Capture and Validation in FMS
topics.description: FMS applies validation rules at entry to prevent bad field data from propagating into the supply plan — what those rules are and what happens when data fails them.

topics.title: What FMS Sends to Planning
topics.description: Yield forecasts and harvest schedules are the key supply signals FMS contributes to Planning software. A stale FMS feed means a stale supply plan.

topics.title: FMS Output to Other Systems
topics.description: Beyond Planning software, FMS feeds logistics, procurement, and reporting systems with field activity and harvest data.

topics.title: Reconciling Field Data with the Plan
topics.description: When planned supply and FMS-reported supply diverge, it needs investigation. How to identify whether the gap is a data lag, a genuine yield miss, or an integration failure.

topics.title: FMS Business Rules
topics.description: The rules that govern how FMS processes field activity — contract compliance checks, quality thresholds, and the conditions that trigger alerts to the planning team.

topics.title: Field Assignment Logic
topics.description: How FMS assigns crop varieties to growers and fields — the rules that match supply commitments to production capacity across the grower network.

topics.title: Yield and Activity Tracking
topics.description: How FMS tracks crop development and generates yield estimates — from planted area and historical yields to in-season updates based on observed crop conditions.

topics.title: Managing Field Activities
topics.description: How field teams use FMS to record planting, crop monitoring, and agronomic interventions — the operational data that drives supply-side visibility.

topics.title: Recording Actuals
topics.description: How harvest actuals are entered in FMS — confirming volumes, quality grades, and delivery dates that feed back into the planning system as supply confirmations.

topics.title: Reporting and Extraction
topics.description: How to extract field data and supply signals from FMS — the reports and exports that planners use to monitor supply position and validate what Planning software is showing.

topics.title: What Is MDM?
topics.description: MDM system is the single source of truth for product master data. Understanding its role — and what breaks without it — is the foundation for working cleanly across Planning software, ERP, and FMS.

topics.title: Why Master Data Management Matters
topics.description: Without a central MDM, every system maintains its own item master and they diverge. The consequences show up as planning errors, reconciliation failures, and data quality incidents.

topics.title: Core MDM Concepts
topics.description: Item master, product hierarchy, data stewardship, and the golden record — the foundational concepts in master data management that planners need to understand.

topics.title: How MDM Structures Data
topics.description: MDM organises master data into entities, attributes, and hierarchies. Understanding this structure explains why changes in MDM propagate the way they do across systems.

topics.title: Key Master Data Entities
topics.description: Items, varieties, product families, units of measure, and planning parameters — the core entities managed in MDM and what each one controls downstream.

topics.title: Data Hierarchies and Relationships
topics.description: How items relate to product families, how BOMs and BODs reference items, and how hierarchy changes ripple through Planning software and ERP.

topics.title: Where Master Data Originates
topics.description: Master data is created by people making product decisions — new product development, supplier onboarding, or organisational restructuring. Understanding who triggers master data creation is the first step to governing it.

topics.title: How Data Enters MDM
topics.description: Master data enters MDM through creation requests, bulk imports, or integration feeds — each with different governance requirements and risk profiles for the downstream planning model.

topics.title: Data Validation in MDM
topics.description: MDM validates new records against completeness rules, duplicate checks, and approval workflows before they are released to downstream systems — what those controls are and what bypassing them costs.

topics.title: What MDM Sends to Planning
topics.description: Items, BOMs, BODs, and planning parameters flow from MDM into Planning software. A change in MDM is the correct way to update these — not a manual override in the planning tool.

topics.title: MDM Output to Other Systems
topics.description: Beyond Planning software, MDM feeds ERP, FMS, and reporting systems. Consistent distribution ensures all systems work from the same product definitions.

topics.title: Keeping Master Data in Sync
topics.description: When systems show different values for the same item, the root cause is almost always an MDM sync issue. How to identify, investigate, and resolve master data mismatches.

topics.title: MDM Governance Rules
topics.description: The rules that govern who can create, change, or retire master data records — and the controls that enforce data quality across the system landscape.

topics.title: Data Matching and Deduplication
topics.description: How MDM identifies and merges duplicate records — preventing the same item from existing under multiple codes across systems.

topics.title: Approval Workflows
topics.description: New records and significant changes in MDM follow approval workflows before distribution. How these workflows are structured and what they protect against.

topics.title: Creating and Maintaining Records
topics.description: How to request, create, and maintain item records in MDM — the process from new product need to active record distributed across systems.

topics.title: Managing Data Changes
topics.description: Changing an attribute, updating a planning parameter, restructuring a hierarchy — how changes are requested, approved, and distributed without disrupting active planning cycles.

topics.title: Auditing and Reporting
topics.description: How to review the change history of a master data record, identify who made a change and when, and generate reports on data quality and completeness.
